notes.note: Tigellius Hermogenes, a Sardinian singer of Caesar's circle and grandson of Phamea, here estranged from Cicero and a source of gossip about Caesar's plans. Later satirized by Horace.
M. Fadius Gallus, a cultivated Epicurean friend of Cicero and art-collector, recipient of several letters in Ad Familiares 7; "Gallus" throughout this letter is the same man.
Phamea, grandfather of Tigellius, whose lawsuit Cicero had agreed to take on; Tigellius's grievance is that Cicero withdrew from the case. The complaint is the source of the present coolness.

notes.anchor: against the young Octavii, sons of Gnaeus
notes.note: The opposing parties in Phamea's suit, sons of a Gnaeus Octavius; not securely identifiable with any Octavius elsewhere in the corpus, so left untagged.

notes.anchor: verdict on our friend Sestius under the Lex Pompeia
notes.note: P. Sestius, the tribune of 57 BC whom Cicero had defended; the trial here turns on the rigid schedule a Pompeian judicial statute imposed on the courts, which clashed with the date set for Phamea's case.
The younger L. Cornelius Balbus, nephew of Caesar's confidant the elder Balbus; "the man" Cicero is careful not to name as the source of the rumour.
